**Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich (2010), "The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis of the Literature," *Journal of Vocational Behavior***
A meta-analysis of 92 samples found pay correlates with job satisfaction at only about r = .15. This supports your friends' view that salary differences (within reasonable bounds) are weak predictors of how happy you'll be.

**Ryan & Deci (2000) — "Self-determination theory and the facilitation of intrinsic motivation, social development, and well-being" (American Psychologist)**

People who believe interests are fixed give up faster when work gets hard; those who believe interest can be developed sustain engagement across domains. This cuts against the "maximize pre-existing interestingness" strategy — interest in new work (like leading a team) can grow.

People systematically overestimate how strongly and how long any single factor will affect their happiness, due to focalism and hedonic adaptation. Whatever feels most salient right now (interestingness, title, salary) will likely matter less than you predict.

**Amabile & Kramer (2011) — "The Power of Small Wins" (Harvard Business Review)**
Analysis of ~12,000 diary entries found the strongest predictor of positive inner work life is making daily progress on meaningful work. The actionable question is which role will give you more frequent, tangible progress — not which sounds more interesting in the abstract.

---

Bottom line on your friends' claim: the literature supports "interesting/intrinsically motivating work beats salary," but **not** "focus on interestingness almost exclusively."
